title: Secure Joint Source-Channel Coding of Multimodal Semantic Sources
summary: We study the problem of secure joint source-channel coding for multimodal semantic sources transmitted over noisy wiretap channels. The source model consists of $m$ modalities (e.g., image, audio, and sensor data), all represented as random variables. The encoder observes independent and identically distributed samples of an arbitrary non-empty subset of modalities. The samples are encoded and transmitted over a discrete memoryless wiretap channel. The legitimate receiver reconstructs all modalities. We extend the rate-distortion-perception problem formulation to multimodal sources. We establish converse and achievability bounds on the fundamental limits of transmission rate, fidelity, and secrecy, under per-modality distortion and perception constraints, and per-subset equivocation constraints. We show that the fundamental limit for secrecy consists of three operationally distinct components: the level of compression, the secret key rate, and the statistics of the wiretap channel.

AI.tldr: This paper studies secure joint source-channel coding for multimodal semantic sources over noisy wiretap channels, establishing fundamental limits on rate, fidelity, and secrecy under per-modality distortion/perception constraints and per-subset equivocation constraints.
AI.motivation: To extend secure communication frameworks to multimodal sources (e.g., image, audio, sensor data) where only a subset of modalities may be observed by the encoder, and to understand the fundamental trade-offs between compression, security, and reconstruction quality in such settings.
AI.method: The authors formulate a rate-distortion-perception problem for multimodal sources, derive converse and achievability bounds, and decompose the secrecy fundamental limit into three operational components: compression level, secret key rate, and wiretap channel statistics.
AI.result: Derived fundamental limits on transmission rate, fidelity, and secrecy under per-modality distortion/perception constraints and per-subset equivocation constraints. Identified that secrecy limit has three distinct components.
AI.conclusion: The fundamental limit for secrecy in multimodal joint source-channel coding consists of three operationally distinct components: the level of compression, the secret key rate, and the statistics of the wiretap channel.

title: Weight Hybrid Architecture of Rydberg-Atomic Sensors
summary: Rydberg atomic quantum receivers have been seen as novel radio frequency measurements and the high sensitivity to a large range of frequencies makes it attractive for communications reception. However, their performance can be significantly degraded by hardware-induced noise, particularly the noise from laser, which impacts the overall system noise floor and exhibits correlation. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a weight hybrid (WH) architecture for Rydberg-atomic sensors, a novel four-channel combining scheme designed for atomic sensors operating in correlated noise environments. By jointly processing dual signal channels and dual noise reference channels, the WH architecture effectively mitigates noise contributions from lasers and other hardware components. All channels are optimally combined via maximum likelihood estimation within an expectation maximization framework, enabling robust signal extraction under correlated noise. Moreover, the proposed WH architecture is universal and can be readily extended to other types of Rydberg receivers to achieve consistent performance improvements.

AI.tldr: Proposes a weight hybrid (WH) architecture with four-channel combining (dual signal + dual noise reference) using EM-based maximum likelihood estimation to mitigate correlated hardware/laser noise in Rydberg atomic quantum receivers.
AI.motivation: Rydberg atomic quantum receivers suffer from hardware-induced noise (especially laser noise) that is correlated and degrades the system noise floor and communication performance.
AI.method: A weight hybrid (WH) architecture with four channels (dual signal + dual noise reference) jointly processed via maximum likelihood estimation within an expectation maximization (EM) framework.
AI.result: The WH architecture effectively mitigates correlated noise from lasers and hardware, enabling robust signal extraction; it is universal and extendable to other Rydberg receivers.
AI.conclusion: The proposed WH architecture is an effective, universal solution for combating correlated hardware noise in Rydberg atomic receivers, with broad applicability.

title: The Geometry of LLM Quantization: GPTQ as Babai's Nearest Plane Algorithm
comment: Published as a conference paper at the Fourteenth International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2026): https://openreview.net/forum?id=NFB4QGGS65
summary: Quantizing the weights of large language models (LLMs) from 16-bit to lower bitwidth is the de facto approach to deploy massive transformers onto more affordable accelerators. While GPTQ emerged as one of the standard methods for one-shot post-training quantization at LLM scale, its inner workings are described as a sequence of algebraic updates that obscure geometric meaning or worst-case guarantees. In this work, we show that, when executed back-to-front (from the last to first dimension) for a linear layer, GPTQ is mathematically identical to Babai's nearest plane algorithm for the classical closest vector problem (CVP) on a lattice defined by the Hessian matrix of the layer's inputs. This equivalence is based on a sophisticated mathematical argument, and has two analytical consequences: first, the GPTQ error propagation step gains an intuitive geometric interpretation; second, GPTQ inherits the error upper bound of Babai's algorithm under the assumption that no weights are clipped. Leveraging this bound, we design post-training quantization methods that avoid clipping, and outperform the original GPTQ. In addition, we provide efficient GPU inference kernels for the resulting representation. Taken together, these results place GPTQ on a firm theoretical footing and open the door to importing decades of progress in lattice algorithms towards the design of future quantization algorithms for billion-parameter models. Source code is available at https://github.com/IST-DASLab/GPTQ-Babai.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that GPTQ, a standard LLM quantization method, is mathematically identical to Babai's nearest plane algorithm for the closest vector problem (CVP) on a lattice defined by the Hessian matrix of layer inputs. This equivalence provides geometric intuition, error bounds, and inspires clipping-free quantization methods that outperform original GPTQ.
AI.motivation: To demystify GPTQ's inner workings, which are described as obscure algebraic updates, and to place it on a firm theoretical footing. The authors aim to understand why GPTQ works well and leverage that understanding to design improved quantization methods.
AI.method: The authors mathematically prove that running GPTQ back-to-front (last to first dimension) on a linear layer is identical to Babai's nearest plane algorithm for solving the closest vector problem on a lattice defined by the Hessian matrix. They then design improved post-training quantization methods that avoid clipping, guided by the error bound inherited from Babai's algorithm, and provide efficient GPU inference kernels.
AI.result: GPTQ is shown to be equivalent to Babai's algorithm under the back-to-front execution. Based on this, the authors derive an error upper bound for GPTQ (assuming no clipping) and develop clipping-free quantization methods that outperform original GPTQ. Efficient GPU inference kernels for the resulting representation are also provided.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a rigorous theoretical foundation for GPTQ by connecting it to lattice algorithms (Babai's nearest plane / CVP), provides geometric interpretation for error propagation, and leverages this insight to create better quantization methods. It opens the door to applying decades of lattice algorithm research to future LLM quantization techniques.

title: Joint Phase Noise and Channel Estimation for OTFS
comment: 13 pages, 17 figures. Accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology. arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2602.12804
summary: This paper investigates the effect of oscillator phase noise in orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS) systems. The paper provides in-depth analysis of the interference due to phase noise in the delay-Doppler domain and derives expressions for SINR for three different oscillator types, namely free-running oscillators, continuous-time phase locked loops (PLLs) and discrete-time PLLs. The analysis demonstrates the OTFS is sensitive to phase noise and requires appropriate estimation and compensation. In particular, the analysis shows phase noise imposed inter-Doppler-interference (IDI) is severe and that existing phase noise estimation techniques which only consider the common-phase-error (CPE) can not compensate this IDI effectively. Additionally, the existing methods in the OTFS literature on phase noise assume the channel to be a known single tap channel. Hence, in this paper, we propose a method for joint channel and phase noise estimation using a Wiener filtering approach. Our proposed method exploits the statistical nature of both the phase noise and the Doppler spread channel. Our numerical results demonstrate the superior performance of our proposed technique, with gains of up to 8~dB in terms of bit error rate (BER) over existing methods in the literature.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes phase noise effects in OTFS systems, shows that existing CPE-based compensation fails due to severe inter-Doppler interference, and proposes a joint channel and phase noise estimation method using Wiener filtering that achieves up to 8 dB BER gain.
AI.motivation: OTFS is sensitive to phase noise, and existing techniques (which only consider common-phase-error and assume known single-tap channels) cannot effectively compensate the severe inter-Doppler interference (IDI) caused by phase noise.
AI.method: The authors derive SINR expressions for three oscillator types (free-running, continuous-time PLL, discrete-time PLL), then propose a joint channel and phase noise estimation method based on Wiener filtering that exploits the statistical properties of both phase noise and Doppler spread channels.
AI.result: The proposed Wiener filtering-based joint estimation method achieves up to 8 dB gain in bit error rate (BER) over existing methods in the literature.
AI.conclusion: OTFS systems require appropriate phase noise estimation and compensation beyond CPE correction; the proposed joint estimation approach significantly outperforms existing techniques.

title: ChannelAgent-Empowered Electromagnetic Space World Model: A Case Study on Agent-Driven Channel Generation for 6G AI-Native Air Interface
summary: As sixth-generation (6G) wireless networks evolve toward increasingly heterogeneous scenarios, tasks, and service requirements, conventional artificial intelligence (AI) models remain limited in task-aware decision-making and autonomous adaptation. To address this issue, this paper first proposes a ChannelAgent-empowered electromagnetic space world model, in which wireless intelligence is organized into a closed-loop process consisting of multi-modal sensing, ChannelAgent as the intelligent core, and execution with feedback update. As a case study, agent-driven channel generation is instantiated through path loss prediction. Specifically, a task-oriented intelligent feature selection mechanism is designed by integrating reinforcement-learning-inspired policy adaptation with evolutionary search, enabling the agent to iteratively derive compact and task-suitable feature subsets according to the current scenario and performance feedback. Simulation results demonstrate superior performance in both single-scenario and multi-scenario tasks, highlighting the potential of the proposed model for autonomous, adaptive, task-oriented, and closed-loop wireless intelligence.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a ChannelAgent-empowered world model for 6G networks that organizes wireless intelligence into a closed-loop process (sensing→ChannelAgent→execution/feedback). As a case study, they apply it to path loss prediction using reinforcement-learning-inspired feature selection with evolutionary search, achieving superior performance in single and multi-scenario tasks.
AI.motivation: Conventional AI models in 6G wireless networks lack task-aware decision-making and autonomous adaptation capabilities needed for increasingly heterogeneous scenarios, tasks, and service requirements.
AI.method: A closed-loop electromagnetic space world model with ChannelAgent as the intelligent core, featuring multi-modal sensing and feedback-driven execution. For the path loss prediction case study, they design a task-oriented feature selection mechanism combining reinforcement-learning-inspired policy adaptation with evolutionary search.
AI.result: Superior performance demonstrated in both single-scenario and multi-scenario tasks for path loss prediction.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ChannelAgent-empowered model shows strong potential for enabling autonomous, adaptive, task-oriented, and closed-loop wireless intelligence in future 6G networks.

title: Making AFDM Secure Against Eavesdroppers: A Phase Function Design Approach
summary: Affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM) has recently emerged as a promising waveform for high-mobility communications due to its resilience to Doppler effects and its advantages for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC). AFDM modulates transmit data symbols using chirp subcarriers with two adjustable parameters. One is used for dealing with the Doppler effect and the second parameter can be used for physical layer security (PLS). In this paper, we focus on designing the second chirp parameter in the form of a generic phase function to enhance the robustness of the waveform against brute-force demodulation by the eavesdropper. In particular, we first derive a design criterion that reveals the brute-force demodulation complexity depends on the first derivative of the phase function. Then, we introduce a family of phase functions that can increase the brute-force demodulation complexity in an unbounded and controllable manner, while preserving chirp structure of AFDM. Our simulation results demonstrate that the proposed phase function design enhances the PLS performance of AFDM by several orders of magnitude compared with the conventional AFDM in terms of brute-force demodulation complexity.

AI.tldr: This paper enhances physical layer security of AFDM by designing the second chirp parameter as a generic phase function, which exponentially increases brute-force demodulation complexity for eavesdroppers while preserving the waveform's chirp structure.
AI.motivation: To improve the physical layer security (PLS) of AFDM against brute-force demodulation attacks by eavesdroppers, addressing the limitation that the conventional AFDM's second chirp parameter offers limited security.
AI.method: The authors derive a design criterion linking brute-force demodulation complexity to the first derivative of the phase function, then propose a family of phase functions that controllably and unboundedly increase this complexity while maintaining the chirp structure of AFDM.
AI.result: The proposed phase function design improves PLS performance by several orders of magnitude compared to conventional AFDM in terms of brute-force demodulation complexity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed phase function approach significantly enhances AFDM's security against eavesdropping, making it a more robust waveform for high-mobility ISAC applications.

title: Transmit Beamforming for High-Rate Underwater Acoustic Communications
summary: Transmit beamforming for underwater acoustic communication is challenging because it requires perfect knowledge of the channel to the receiver in advance. In practice, channel estimates must be learned through feedback and are often noisy or outdated because of feedback delay and channel variation. In this paper, we investigate angle-based beamforming strategies for a single-user link that reduce dependence on full channel knowledge by exploiting stable components of the geometric structure in the propagation field. In particular, we focus on scenarios in which there exists a dominant path that remains relatively stable over time, making it a suitable candidate for transmit beamforming. Experimental results using the SPACE and MACE data sets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method in terms of data-detection mean-squared error and bit error rate.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes angle-based beamforming for underwater acoustic communication that exploits stable dominant propagation paths, reducing reliance on full channel knowledge, and validates the approach using SPACE and MACE experimental data.
AI.motivation: Traditional transmit beamforming for underwater acoustic communication requires perfect, up-to-date channel knowledge, which is hard to obtain due to feedback delay and channel variation. The authors aim to reduce dependency on full channel state information.
AI.method: The authors investigate angle-based beamforming strategies that leverage stable geometric structure components—specifically a dominant propagation path that remains relatively stable over time—to avoid reliance on noisy or outdated full-channel estimates.
AI.result: Experimental validation using SPACE and MACE datasets shows the proposed method achieves good performance in terms of data-detection mean-squared error (MSE) and bit error rate (BER).
AI.conclusion: Angle-based beamforming exploiting stable dominant paths is an effective and practical alternative to full-channel-knowledge-dependent beamforming for underwater acoustic communication.

title: Downlink Performance Analysis of Pinching Antenna Systems: WDMA or NOMA?
summary: This paper presents an analytical framework for downlink pinching antenna systems (PASS) employing waveguide division multiple access (WDMA) and non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA). A unified channel model is developed to capture antenna deployment, user spatial distribution, and path loss. Closed-form and single-integral expressions for the outage probability and average achievable rate are derived and validated via Monte Carlo simulations. The results show that NOMA achieves higher spectral efficiency at high transmit signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) due to successive interference cancellation (SIC), whereas WDMA offers more reliable performance at low to moderate SNR but suffers from an outage floor and rate saturation at high SNR. Moreover, WDMA performance is more sensitive to the user spatial distribution due to the spatially dependent inter-waveguide interference. These findings provide design insights for access-scheme selection and antenna placement in PASS.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an analytical framework for pinching antenna systems (PASS) comparing WDMA and NOMA, finding NOMA better at high SNR and WDMA more reliable at low/moderate SNR but with outage floors.
AI.motivation: To provide an analytical framework and design insights for access-scheme selection (WDMA vs. NOMA) and antenna placement in downlink pinching antenna systems (PASS).
AI.method: A unified channel model incorporating antenna deployment, user spatial distribution, and path loss. Closed-form and single-integral expressions for outage probability and average achievable rate derived and validated via Monte Carlo simulations.
AI.result: NOMA achieves higher spectral efficiency at high SNR due to SIC; WDMA offers better reliability at low-to-moderate SNR but suffers from outage floor and rate saturation at high SNR; WDMA is more sensitive to user spatial distribution due to inter-waveguide interference.
AI.conclusion: These findings offer design insights for choosing between WDMA and NOMA and for antenna placement in PASS systems.

title: RAVEN: Real-time Autoregressive Video Extrapolation with Consistency-model GRPO
summary: Causal autoregressive video diffusion models support real-time streaming generation by extrapolating future chunks from previously generated content. Distilling such generators from high-fidelity bidirectional teachers yields competitive few-step models, yet a persistent gap between the history distributions encountered during training and those arising at inference constrains generation quality over long horizons. We introduce the Real-time Autoregressive Video Extrapolation Network (RAVEN), a training-time test framework that repacks each self rollout into an interleaved sequence of clean historical endpoints and noisy denoising states. This formulation aligns training attention with inference-time extrapolation and allows downstream chunk losses to supervise the history representations on which future predictions depend. We further propose Consistency-model Group Relative Policy Optimization (CM-GRPO), which reformulates a consistency sampling step as a conditional Gaussian transition and applies online Reinforcement Learning (RL) directly to this kernel, avoiding the Euler-Maruyama auxiliary process adopted in prior flow-model RL formulations. Experiments demonstrate that RAVEN surpasses recent causal video distillation baselines across quality, semantic, and dynamic degree evaluations, and that CM-GRPO provides further gains when combined with RAVEN.

AI.tldr: RAVEN is a training-time test framework for causal autoregressive video diffusion models that aligns training attention with inference extrapolation by repacking rollouts into interleaved clean/noisy sequences. CM-GRPO applies online RL directly to consistency sampling steps, improving video generation quality over long horizons.
AI.motivation: There is a persistent distribution gap between history distributions seen during training and those at inference time, which constrains generation quality over long horizons in causal autoregressive video diffusion models.
AI.method: (1) RAVEN: repacks each self-rollout into interleaved clean historical endpoints and noisy denoising states to align training with inference. (2) CM-GRPO: reformulates consistency sampling as conditional Gaussian transition and applies online RL directly, avoiding Euler-Maruyama auxiliary processes.
AI.result: RAVEN surpasses recent causal video distillation baselines across quality, semantic, and dynamic degree evaluations. CM-GRPO provides further improvements when combined with RAVEN.
AI.conclusion: Both RAVEN and CM-GRPO effectively address the history distribution mismatch and improve long-horizon generation quality in causal autoregressive video diffusion models.
